title: Heart Rate Response Patterns during Different Stages of Exercise Electrocardiography
desc: Across 75 healthy adults, only ten showed a linear heart-rate rise through the Bruce protocol — eleven distinct response patterns emerged, pointing to an intrinsic maximal heart rate unique to each person.

title: Heart Rate Recovery Patterns Following Treadmill Test in Normal Individuals
desc: Heart-rate recovery after a treadmill test in 95 healthy individuals falls into three distinct patterns, though all drop ≥50 bpm within the first three minutes — a normative baseline for prognostic work.

title: Correlation of Heart Rate Response with Severity of Myocardial Ischemia during Treadmill Testing
desc: In 81 patients with effort angina, chronotropic incompetence proved a highly specific marker of severe exercise-induced ischemia; pairing it with blunted first-minute heart-rate recovery improved diagnostic accuracy.

title: Females over Fifty Need a Different Regression Equation for Age-Predicted Maximal Heart Rate
desc: Stage-wise analysis of 70 normal individuals shows women over fifty rarely reach Bruce stage 4 and peak lower — so the standard 220−age formula needs a sex- and age-specific regression equation.

title: Effect of ECG Changes on Standing and Hyperventilation Prior to Exercise Testing
desc: In 100 subjects, ECG changes provoked simply by standing and hyperventilating before a treadmill test can mimic or unmask exercise-induced ischemia, and must be accounted for when reading the test.

title: Early Impaired LV Relaxation in Uncomplicated Prehypertension Detected by Exercise ECG
desc: Nearly half of people with isolated, uncomplicated prehypertension have impaired left-ventricular relaxation even without hypertrophy — detectable non-invasively through the exercise-induced V1 P-wave terminal deflection.

title: Effect of Treadmill Testing (Bruce Protocol) on the P Wave in Normal Individuals
desc: Charts how the P wave evolves through a Bruce-protocol treadmill test in normal individuals — rising early, merging into the PR segment at peak exercise, then re-emerging during recovery.

title: Effect of Pre-Exercise Heart-Rate Increase on Subsequent and Maximal Heart Rate during Treadmill Testing
desc: In 63 normal subjects, a large pre-exercise heart-rate rise (from standing + hyperventilation) blunts the subsequent increase in the early Bruce stages — a confounder for interpreting stress tests.

title: Status of Exercise Duration and Metabolic Equivalents in Interpretation of Exercise ECG
desc: Argues that exercise duration and metabolic equivalents reflect overall effort tolerance, which depends on many non-cardiac factors — so reduced tolerance alone shouldn't be read as the severity of coronary disease.

title: Objective ECG Documentation of ‘Walk-Through Angina’ during Treadmill Testing
desc: Two cases of ‘walk-through angina’ — angina easing despite continued exertion — documented objectively, with the symptomatic relief mirrored by a measurable reduction in ischemic ECG changes.

title: T-Wave Alternans Preceding ST Depression as a Sign of Myocardial Ischemia during Treadmill Test
desc: A case in which T-wave alternans appeared before classical ischemic ST-segment depression during a treadmill test, suggesting it can serve as an early electrical marker of myocardial ischemia.

title: Pseudo ST Elevation & Terminal T-Wave Deflection during Treadmill Test from Printer Damping
desc: Six asymptomatic individuals showed ST elevation with terminal negative T waves in V1–V3 on a treadmill test that proved to be a printer-damping artifact — normal on another machine and on echo.

title: Overdamping of Printer Styli can Simulate Myocardial Ischemia during Exercise ECG
desc: Four ‘ischemic-looking’ treadmill tests turned out to be artifacts: over-damping of the printer's styli produced pseudo ST-segment deviation in asymptomatic, low-risk individuals with normal echocardiograms.
